instructions: Rate each formula independently on four dimensions using a 1-5 scale. Use ONLY the variable definitions and task context provided. Do not reward or penalize based on how the formula was produced; method identity, R^2 and any prior explanation are intentionally withheld.

dimensions.title: Domain meaning of variables and their combination

anchors.1: Variable combinations are meaningless or physically contradictory; no plausible domain reading.
anchors.2: A few terms are readable but most combinations lack domain justification.
anchors.3: About half the structure has a plausible domain reading; the rest is unclear.
anchors.4: Most variable combinations map to sensible domain quantities with minor gaps.
anchors.5: Every combination maps cleanly to a recognizable, domain-meaningful quantity.

dimensions.title: Structural plausibility against basic scientific common sense

anchors.1: Structure violates obvious scientific common sense (e.g. adding incompatible quantities everywhere).
anchors.2: Several structural choices look forced or arbitrary.
anchors.3: Structure is neither clearly sound nor clearly wrong.
anchors.4: Structure is mostly consistent with common sense; one or two questionable parts.
anchors.5: Structure is fully consistent with basic scientific reasoning; no forced relationships.

anchors.1: Unreadable; cannot be summarized or generalized by a human in reasonable time.
anchors.2: Very hard to read; only isolated parts can be summarized.
anchors.3: Readable with effort; partial generalization possible.
anchors.4: Fairly readable and largely generalizable with minor effort.
anchors.5: Immediately readable and easy to state as a general relationship.

dimensions.title: Support for a discussable, testable hypothesis

anchors.1: Supports no discussable or testable hypothesis.
anchors.2: Suggests only a vague, hard-to-test idea.
anchors.3: Supports one weak but discussable hypothesis.
anchors.4: Supports a clear hypothesis that could be discussed and partially tested.
anchors.5: Directly suggests a clear, testable hypothesis a domain expert could act on.
